faqs.a: Radical acceptance is a DBT skill for accepting reality fully, exactly as it is, especially the parts you can't change. The idea is that fighting an unchangeable fact adds suffering on top of pain, and accepting what's real lowers that added layer.

faqs.q: Does radical acceptance mean approving of what happened?
faqs.a: No. Acceptance isn't agreement, approval, or giving up. You can radically accept that something painful or unjust happened while still wishing it were different and still working to change what can be changed.

It's especially useful with loss, chronic illness, or situations that can't be undone, where the only thing left to change is your relationship to the fact itself. It's a practice that usually has to be renewed, and it tends to get easier with time.
